edibility: Mycelium itself isn't typically eaten; the fruiting bodies (Honey Mushrooms) are edible only after thorough cooking. Raw fruiting bodies contain a thermolabile toxin that causes gastrointestinal distress.
taste: Mycelium tastes like raw mushroom skin with earthy notes. When cooked, the fruiting body has a mild, earthy flavor with a slightly chewy texture.
search: The mycelium is found by digging in the soil and wood of infected trees — bioluminescent rhizomorphs ('foxfire') are occasionally visible in dark forests at night. Fruiting bodies appear in autumn at the base of dying conifers and hardwoods, growing in dense clusters.

toxicity.symptoms: Raw fruit body causes gastrointestinal distress including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea; mycelium not normally eaten
